questions.question: In scaled dot-product attention, why is the dot product divided by sqrt(d_k) before the softmax?
options: The division by sqrt(d_k) normalizes the attention weights to sum to 1.

When d_k is large, dot products between query and key vectors grow large in magnitude, causing the softmax to produce near-one-hot outputs (extreme focus on one token). This creates extremely small gradients for most tokens — the vanishing gradient problem in attention. Dividing by sqrt(d_k) keeps the dot products in a regime where softmax gives more diffuse, useful attention distributions.
Dividing by sqrt(d_k) converts the attention scores to probability values in [0, 1].
The scaling prevents the attention matrix from becoming asymmetric.
questions.answer: When d_k is large, dot products between query and key vectors grow large in magnitude, causing the softmax to produce near-one-hot outputs (extreme focus on one token). This creates extremely small gradients for most tokens — the vanishing gradient problem in attention. Dividing by sqrt(d_k) keeps the dot products in a regime where softmax gives more diffuse, useful attention distributions.
questions.explanation: For d_k=512: without scaling, attention score = q·k ≈ 512 (assuming unit vectors) → softmax([512, 1, 3]) ≈ [1.0, 0.0, 0.0]. Only one token gets gradient. With scaling: 512/√512=22.6, softmax([22.6, 0.04, 0.13]) still somewhat peaked but trainable.

questions.question: What specific disadvantage does BERT (encoder-only) have compared to GPT (decoder-only) that makes BERT inappropriate for text generation tasks?
options: BERT is too large to run efficiently for text generation.

BERT uses bidirectional attention — every token sees all other tokens in both directions. For generation, each token must be predicted using ONLY preceding tokens (autoregressive). BERT has no mechanism for this constraint: it cannot generate text because it was trained to fill masks using full bidirectional context, not to predict the next token from partial context.
BERT does not use the Transformer architecture internally.
BERT requires the full sequence length to be known before processing begins.
questions.answer: BERT uses bidirectional attention — every token sees all other tokens in both directions. For generation, each token must be predicted using ONLY preceding tokens (autoregressive). BERT has no mechanism for this constraint: it cannot generate text because it was trained to fill masks using full bidirectional context, not to predict the next token from partial context.
questions.explanation: The bidirectionality that makes BERT excellent at understanding (it reads the full context) is exactly what prevents it from generating coherently. GPT's causal mask ensures token i can only attend to tokens 1..i, making autoregressive generation natural.

questions.question: Multi-head attention uses 8 separate attention 'heads' that each operate on a d_model/8 dimensional subspace. What advantage does this provide over single-head attention with the full d_model dimension?
options: Multi-head attention is 8x faster than single-head attention at the same d_model.

Different attention heads learn to capture different types of relationships simultaneously. One head may attend to syntactic dependencies (subject-verb agreement), another to semantic relationships (synonyms and related concepts), another to positional proximity. Single-head attention must compromise across all relationship types in one learned representation — multi-head allows specialization.
Multi-head attention prevents the model from attending to padding tokens.
Using 8 heads reduces memory usage by a factor of 8 compared to single-head attention.
questions.answer: Different attention heads learn to capture different types of relationships simultaneously. One head may attend to syntactic dependencies (subject-verb agreement), another to semantic relationships (synonyms and related concepts), another to positional proximity. Single-head attention must compromise across all relationship types in one learned representation — multi-head allows specialization.
questions.explanation: Visualizing BERT attention heads: Head 1 often attends to the previous token (local context). Head 2 attends to the end of the sentence (global structure). Head 5 tracks coreference (connecting 'it' to its antecedent noun). This specialization is impossible with a single attention head.
